revision.text:
[[arz:ضريح الاماميه]][[de:Imamiya-Schrein]][[en:Imamiya Shrine]][[fr:Imamiya-jinja]][[ja:今宮神社 (京都市)]][[my:အိမမိယနတ်ကျောင်း]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Imamiya Shrine
今宮神社 | image = File:Imamiya Shrine gate.JPG | caption = Imamiya Shrine's main gate | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.04577778|N|135.74205278|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = 994 | date_destroyed = | location = 21, Imamiya-cho, Murasakino
{{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku|1=Kita-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} 603-8231 | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Kita-ku|qid=Q1154970|1=Kita-ku, Kyoto}} }} {{nihongo|'''Imamiya Shrine'''|今宮神社|Imamiya-jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kita-ku, Kyoto|en|Kita-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1154970|lt=Kita-ku, Kyoto|1=Kita-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was originally established for patrons to pray for safety from an {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|qid=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}, though it has evolved into a shrine where patrons can pray for general good health. The shrine complex is embedded in a forest of large trees northwest of {{ill|Daitoku-ji|en|Daitoku-ji|qid=Q901256|lt=Daitoku-ji|1=Daitoku-ji}} and includes many minor shrines in addition to the main shrine, or ''[[honden]]''.{{cite web|url=http://www.kyotoguide.com/ver2/walking/walking-oldcapitalwalking.html |title=Kyoto Walking map |accessdate=2008-05-12 |work=Kyoto Visitor's Guide}} On every second Sunday in April, one of the 3 major [[Japanese festivals|festivals]] in Kyoto, [[Yasurai Matsuri]], is held at the shrine. The word {{nihongo|''imamiya''|今宮}} means "newly constructed." ==History== Imamiya Shrine was founded during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} in 994. In 1001, however, the shrine was moved to its present-day location as a response to an {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|qid=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}} that had hit the {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} area a year earlier. Current iterations of many of the structures were rebuilt in 1902.{{cite web|url=http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/ecfa8fdd6a53a7fc4925700e00303ed8/b281cc0ba924bb714925726f0010b7fb!OpenDocument |title=Imamiya Shrine |accessdate=2008-05-12 |work=Learning about Kyoto |author=Kawaguchi, Satoko}} The shrine's festival, [[Yasurai Matsuri]], was also created as a response to the epidemic. Through music and dance, the festival attempts to appease {{ill|Petrel|en|Petrel|qid=Q3092053|lt=Petrel|1=Petrel}}s around Kyoto with {{ill|Cherry blossom|en|Cherry blossom|qid=Q871991|lt=Cherry blossom|1=Cherry blossom}} petals in their beaks, which were then believed to be spreading disease. ==Attractions== There are several attractions that are unique to Imamiya Shrine. Specifically, there are two longstanding restaurants adjacent to the shrine. These shops' specialty are ''aburimochi'' - skewered, roasted rice cakes that are a traditional Kyoto confection. The two restaurants, named {{ill|Ichimonjiya Wasuke|en|Ichimonjiya Wasuke|qid=Q30590108|lt=Ichiwa|1=Ichimonjiya Wasuke}} and Kazariya, have been open since 1000 and 1656 respectively and are located immediately outside the shrine's east gate.{{cite web|url=http://www.kyopro.kufs.ac.jp/dp/dp01.nsf/b7eb328e75d9627a49256feb00103b33/133c5fb520e8d78f4925721400151d70!OpenDocument |title=Aburimochi |accessdate=2008-05-12 |work=Learning About Kyoto |author=Imanaka, Chiaki}} The shrine also houses a rock called ''ahokashisan''. The stone is believed to possess magical properties. If a person rubs the stone and then rubs an injured area of their body, it is said that they will heal quicker than normal. Furthermore, if a person taps the stone three times, then lifts it, the stone will feel heavy. Afterwards, if the same person strokes the stone three times while making a wish and then lifts it for a second time and the stone feels light, it is said that their wish will be granted. The ''ahokashisan'', as well as [[Yasurai Matsuri]], are designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. ==See also== {{Commons Category|Imamiya-jinja (Kita-ku, Kyoto)|Imamiya Shrine}} *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto|List of Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] ==References== {{reflist}} [[en:Imamiya Shrine]] [[arz:ضريح الاماميه]] [[de:Imamiya-Schrein]] [[fr:Imamiya-jinja]] [[ja:今宮神社 (京都市)]] [[my:အိမမိယနတ်ကျောင်း]] {{wikidata link|Q500955}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
